content:
# Exodus Chapter 18 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~43r–44r (PDF pp. 82–83); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 59–60 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_018_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_018_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_018_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## Jethro Hears and Comes to Moses (vv. 1–6)

**1** And Jethro [**Yitro**], priest of Midian [**kohen Midyan**], father-in-law of Moses [**choten Mosheh**], heard [**vayyishma**] all [**et kol**] that God [**Elohim**] had done [**asah**] for Moses and for Israel His people [**ule'Yisra'el ammo**] — that the LORD [**ki-hotsi YHWH**] had brought out [**hotsi**] Israel from Egypt [**et-Yisra'el mimMitsrayim**].

**2** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, took [**vayyiqqach**] Zipporah [**et-Tsipporah**], the wife of Moses [**eshet Mosheh**], after he had sent her away [**achar shillucheyha**],

**3** and her two sons [**ve'et sheney vaneyha**], of whom the name of the one [**asher shem ha'echad**] was Gershom [**Gershom**], for he said, "I have been a sojourner [**ger hayiti**] in a foreign land [**be'erets nokhriyyah**],"

**4** and the name of the other [**veshem ha'echad**] was Eliezer [**Eli'ezer**], "for the God of my father [**ki-Elohey avi**] was my help [**be'ezri**], and He delivered me [**vayyatsileni**] from the sword of Pharaoh [**mecherev Par'oh**]."

**5** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, came [**vayyavo**] — and his sons [**uvanav**] and his wife [**ve'ishto**] — to Moses, to the wilderness [**el-hamidbar**] where he was encamped [**asher-hu choneh sham**], at the mountain of God [**el har ha'Elohim**].

**6** And he said [**vayyomer**] to Moses, "I, your father-in-law Jethro [**ani chotenekha Yitro**], am coming [**ba**] to you, and your wife [**ve'ishtekha**] and her two sons [**ushney vaneyha**] with her [**immah**]."

## Moses Receives Jethro and Recounts the Exodus (vv. 7–12)

**7** And Moses went out [**vayyetse**] to meet [**liqrat**] his father-in-law, and he bowed down [**vayyishtachu**] and he kissed him [**vayyishaq-lo**]. And they asked [**vayyish'alu**] each man [**ish**] of his companion [**lere'ehu**] concerning peace [**leshalom**]. And they came [**vayyavo'u**] into the tent [**ha'ohelah**].

**8** And Moses recounted [**vayesapper**] to his father-in-law all [**et kol**] that the LORD had done [**asher-asah YHWH**] to Pharaoh and to Egypt [**lePar'oh uleMitsrayim**] on account of [**al odot**] Israel, all [**et kol**] the hardship [**hattela'ah**] that had found them [**asher metsa'atam**] on the way [**badderekh**], and the LORD had delivered them [**vayyatsilem YHWH**].

**9** And Jethro rejoiced [**vayyichad**] over all [**al kol**] the good [**hattovah**] that the LORD had done [**asher-asah YHWH**] for Israel, that He had delivered them [**asher hitsilo**] from the hand of Egypt [**miyyad Mitsrayim**].

**10** And Jethro said [**vayyomer Yitro**], "Blessed be [**barukh**] the LORD [**YHWH**], who delivered you [**asher hitsil etkhem**] from the hand of Egypt [**miyyad Mitsrayim**] and from the hand of Pharaoh [**umiyyad Par'oh**], who delivered [**asher hitsil**] the people [**et-ha'am**] from under the hand of Egypt [**mittachat yad-Mitsrayim**].

**11** Now I know [**attah yada'ti**] that the LORD is greater [**ki-gadol YHWH**] than all the gods [**mikkol-ha'elohim**], indeed, in the matter [**ki vaddavar**] in which they acted presumptuously [**asher zadu**] against them [**aleyhem**]."

**12** And Jethro, father-in-law of Moses, took [**vayyiqqach**] a burnt offering [**olah**] and sacrifices [**uzevachim**] for God [**le'lohim**]. And Aaron came [**vayyavo Aharon**], and all the elders of Israel [**vekhol ziqney Yisra'el**], to eat bread [**le'ekhol-lechem**] with the father-in-law of Moses [**im-choten Mosheh**] before God [**lifney ha'Elohim**].

## Moses Judges the People Alone (vv. 13–16)

**13** And it came to pass [**vayehi**] on the next day [**mimmocharot**], that Moses sat [**vayyeshev Mosheh**] to judge [**lishpot**] the people. And the people stood [**vayyya'amod ha'am**] over Moses [**al-Mosheh**] from morning [**min-habboqer**] until evening [**ad-ha'arev**].

**14** And the father-in-law of Moses saw [**vayyar**] all [**et kol**] that he was doing [**asher-hu oseh**] for the people. And he said, "What is this thing [**mah-haddavar hazzeh**] that you are doing [**asher attah oseh**] for the people? Why do you sit [**maddua attah yoshev**] alone [**levaddekha**], and all the people stand [**vekhol-ha'am nitsav**] over you [**alekha**] from morning until evening [**min-boqer ad-erev**]?"

**15** And Moses said to his father-in-law, "Because [**ki**] the people come [**yavo**] to me to inquire of [**lidrosh**] God [**Elohim**].

**16** When they have a matter [**ki-yihyeh lahem davar**], it comes [**ba**] to me, and I judge [**veshafat'ti**] between a man and his companion [**beyn ish uveyn re'ehu**], and I make known [**vehodat'ti**] the statutes of God [**et-chuqqey ha'Elohim**] and His laws [**ve'et-torotav**]."

## Jethro's Counsel: Delegation of Judgment (vv. 17–23)

**17** And the father-in-law of Moses said to him, "The thing [**haddavar**] that you are doing [**asher attah oseh**] is not good [**lo-tov**].

**18** You will surely wear out [**navol tibbol**] — both you [**gam-attah**] and also [**gam**] this people [**ha'am hazzeh**] that is with you [**asher immakh**] — for the thing [**ki-khaved**] is too heavy [**khaved**] for you [**mimmekha haddavar**]; you are not able [**lo-tukhal**] to do it [**asohu**] alone [**levaddekha**].

**19** Now listen [**attah shema**] to my voice [**beqoli**]; I will counsel you [**i'atsekha**], and may God be [**vihi Elohim**] with you [**immakh**]. You be [**heyeh attah**] for the people [**la'am**] before God [**mul ha'Elohim**], and you bring [**veheveyta attah**] the matters [**et-haddevarim**] to God [**el-ha'Elohim**].

**20** And you shall warn them [**vehizhar'tah ethem**] concerning the statutes [**et-hachuqqim**] and the laws [**ve'et-hattorot**], and you shall make known to them [**vehodata lahem**] the way [**et-hadderekh**] in which they must walk [**yelkhu vah**] and the work [**ve'et-hamma'aseh**] that they must do [**asher ya'asun**].

**21** And you shall see [**vechatah**] out of all the people [**mikkol-ha'am**] men of ability [**anshey-chayil**], fearers of God [**yir'ey Elohim**], men of truth [**anshey emet**], haters of unjust gain [**son'ey vatsa**]. And you shall set [**vesamta**] over them [**aleyhem**] rulers of thousands [**sarey alafim**], rulers of hundreds [**sarey me'ot**], rulers of fifties [**sarey chamishim**], and rulers of tens [**vesarey asarot**].

**22** And they shall judge [**veshaftu**] the people at all times [**et-ha'am bekhol-et**]. And it shall be [**vehayah**] that every great matter [**kol-haddavar haggadol**] they shall bring [**yavi'u**] to you, and every small matter [**vekhol-haddavar haqqaton**] they shall judge [**yishpetu**] themselves. And make it light [**vehaqqel**] upon yourself [**me'alekha**], and they shall bear [**venase'u**] with you [**ittakh**].

**23** If you do [**im et-haddavar hazzeh ta'aseh**] this thing, and God commands you [**vetsivvekha Elohim**], then you will be able to endure [**veyakholta amod**], and also [**vegam**] all this people [**kol-ha'am hazzeh**] shall go [**yavo**] to its place [**al-meqomo**] in peace [**beshalom**]."

## Moses Implements the Judicial Reform (vv. 24–27)

**24** And Moses listened [**vayyishma Mosheh**] to the voice of [**leqol**] his father-in-law, and he did [**vayyya'as**] all [**kol**] that he had said [**asher amar**].

**25** And Moses chose [**vayyivchar Mosheh**] men of ability [**anshey-chayil**] out of all Israel [**mikkol-Yisra'el**], and he gave [**vayyitten**] them [**otam**] as heads [**rashim**] over the people [**al-ha'am**] — rulers of thousands [**sarey alafim**], rulers of hundreds [**sarey me'ot**], rulers of fifties [**sarey chamishim**], and rulers of tens [**vesarey asarot**].

**26** And they judged [**veshaftu**] the people at all times [**et-ha'am bekhol-et**]. The hard matter [**et-haddavar haqqasheh**] they would bring [**yevi'un**] to Moses, and every small matter [**vekhol-haddavar haqqaton**] they would judge [**yishpetu**] themselves.

**27** And Moses sent away [**vayeshalach Mosheh**] his father-in-law, and he went [**vayyyelekh**] to him [**lo**], to his land [**el-artso**].

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations
The Leningrad Codex folios ~43r–44r (PDF pp. 82–83) contain the entirety of Exodus 18. On page 82 (the first image), the text of Exodus 17 concludes in the upper-left portion of the folio, and Exodus 18 begins in the right column, with the chapter opening marked by a petuchah (open paragraph) division before 18:1 — a major section break signaling the transition from the Amalek battle narrative to the Jethro visit, a shift from warfare to governance. The three-column prose layout continues throughout. Page 83 (the second image) carries the chapter through to its conclusion, with Jethro's counsel and Moses' implementation of the judicial reform.

A petuchah (open paragraph) marker appears before 18:1, marking the transition from the Amalek battle and memorial (17:14–16) to the Jethro narrative — a major shift in setting, characters, and theme. A setumah (closed paragraph) marker appears before v. 5, separating the background information about Zipporah and the sons (vv. 2–4) from Jethro's arrival at the wilderness camp. Another setumah appears before v. 13, marking the transition from the worship meal (v. 12) to the judicial scene that motivates Jethro's counsel. A petuchah appears before v. 17 or thereabout, marking Jethro's speech of counsel as a distinct unit. These paragraph divisions structure the chapter into its four major units: Jethro's arrival and reunion (vv. 1–7), Moses' recounting and Jethro's confession (vv. 8–12), the judicial problem (vv. 13–16), and Jethro's solution and its implementation (vv. 17–27).

The masorah parva annotations are visible throughout the folios. Annotations appear on Yitro (v. 1, Jethro's name — appearing with this spelling primarily in Exodus 18; he is called Re'u'el in Exod. 2:18, Chovav in Judg. 4:11, and the relationship between these names has generated extensive discussion), on choten Mosheh (vv. 1, 2, 5, 6, 12, 14, 15, 17, 24, 27 — the phrase "father-in-law of Moses" appears with extraordinary frequency in this chapter, thirteen times, as though the narrator is insistently identifying Jethro through his relationship to Moses), on achar shillucheyha (v. 2, "after her being sent away" — a masorah note flagging this phrase, which implies a dismissal or divorce that is narrated nowhere else in the text; the verb shillach in the Piel means "to send away, to dismiss, to release," and in legal contexts can mean "to divorce"), on Gershom (v. 3, the name connected to the folk etymology ger hayiti, "I was a sojourner"), on Eli'ezer (v. 4, the name meaning "my God is help" — mentioned only here and in 1 Chron. 23:15, 17 among Moses' sons), on vayyichad (v. 9, "he rejoiced" — a rare verbal form, possibly from chadah, "to rejoice," occurring only here in this form in the Hebrew Bible, generating a masorah note on its uniqueness), on navol tibbol (v. 18, "you will surely wear out" — the infinitive absolute construction using naval, "to wilt, to wither, to wear out"), on anshey-chayil (v. 21, "men of ability/valor" — chayil being a term with a wide semantic range encompassing military prowess, wealth, moral excellence, and administrative competence), on sarey alafim (v. 21, "rulers of thousands" — the military-administrative hierarchy), and on el-artso (v. 27, "to his land" — Jethro's return to Midian). The cantillation on v. 11 places distinctive emphasis on attah yada'ti ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim ("Now I know that the LORD is greater than all the gods"), marking this as the theological climax of Jethro's confession. The cantillation on v. 21 distributes the four qualifications of the judges — anshey-chayil, yir'ey Elohim, anshey emet, son'ey vatsa — with careful rhythmic balance.

The Codex Vaticanus pages 59–60 preserve the LXX text of Exodus 18. The text begins on page 59 in the third column of the three-column continuous uncial script, carrying over from the conclusion of chapter 17. At v. 1, the LXX renders Yitro as Iothor and kohen Midyan as ho hiereus Madiam ("the priest of Midian"), using hiereus ("priest") for kohen. At v. 2, the LXX renders achar shillucheyha as meta tēn aphesin autēs ("after her release/dismissal"), using aphesis ("release, dismissal, sending away") for shillucheyha — the same word used for the "release" of debts in the jubilee and, in the New Testament, for the "forgiveness" of sins. At v. 3, the LXX renders ger hayiti as paroikos ("sojourner, resident alien") for ger. At v. 4, for Eli'ezer, the LXX renders the name explanation as ho theos tou patros mou boēthos mou ("the God of my father is my helper"), using boēthos ("helper") for be'ezri. At v. 5, the LXX renders har ha'Elohim as to oros tou theou ("the mountain of God"), using oros ("mountain") for har. At v. 7, the LXX renders vayyishtachu as prosekunēsen autō ("he did obeisance to him"), using proskuneō ("to prostrate oneself, to worship") for vayyishtachu — the same verb used in the New Testament for worship of God. At v. 9, the LXX renders vayyichad as exestē ("he was amazed, he was beside himself") rather than "he rejoiced" — interpreting the rare Hebrew verb chadah differently, reading astonishment rather than joy. At v. 11, the LXX renders ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim as hoti megas kurios para pantas tous theous ("because the Lord is great beyond all the gods"), using para with the accusative ("beyond, above") for min ("from, more than"). The phrase ki vaddavar asher zadu aleyhem is rendered dioti epethento autois ("because they set upon them"), apparently reading zadu differently — the MT's "they acted presumptuously" becomes the LXX's "they attacked them." At v. 12, the LXX renders olah uzevachim as holokautōmata kai thusias ("burnt offerings and sacrifices"), using holokautōma ("whole burnt offering") for olah and thusia ("sacrifice") for zevach. The phrase le'ekhol-lechem... lifney ha'Elohim is rendered phagein arton... enantion tou theou ("to eat bread... before God"), preserving the Hebrew closely. At v. 17, the LXX renders lo-tov as ou orthōs ("not rightly"), using orthōs ("correctly, rightly") rather than kalos ("well, good") — a shift from moral evaluation to procedural correctness. At v. 18, the LXX renders navol tibbol as phthora kataphthareisē ("with wearing out you will wear out"), preserving the infinitive absolute construction with a cognate noun. At v. 21, the LXX renders anshey-chayil as andras dunatous ("capable/powerful men"), using dunatos ("powerful, capable") for chayil; yir'ey Elohim as theosebeis ("God-fearing"), using theosebēs; anshey emet as andras dikaious ("righteous men"), rendering emet ("truth") as dikaios ("just, righteous"); and son'ey vatsa as misountas huperēphanian ("hating arrogance"), rendering betsa ("unjust gain, profit") as huperēphania ("arrogance, pride") — a significant interpretive shift from greed to pride. At v. 23, the LXX adds a phrase not in the MT: kai dunēsē parastēnai ("and you will be able to stand"), rendering veyakholta amod. At v. 27, the LXX renders el-artso as eis tēn gēn autou ("into his land"), preserving the Hebrew closely.

### Key Translation Decisions

1. **"Jethro, priest of Midian, father-in-law of Moses" (v. 1) — Yitro kohen Midyan choten Mosheh:** Hebrew vayyishma Yitro kohen Midyan choten Mosheh et kol-asher asah Elohim leMosheh ule'Yisra'el ammo ki-hotsi YHWH et-Yisra'el mimMitsrayim. The name Yitro (Jethro) appears in the Hebrew Bible exclusively in Exodus 18 (and the parallel in Judg. 1:16 in some traditions). The same figure appears to be called Re'u'el ("friend of God" or "shepherd of God") in Exodus 2:18, Chovav in Numbers 10:29 and Judges 4:11, and possibly also by other designations. The relationship between these names — whether they represent different names for one person, different persons, or different textual traditions — remains one of the enduring puzzles of Pentateuchal criticism. The title kohen Midyan ("priest of Midian") identifies Jethro as a cultic functionary of a non-Israelite people. The noun kohen ("priest") is used both for Israelite priests (the Aaronic priesthood) and for pagan priests (e.g., Potiphera kohen On, Gen. 41:45). Jethro's priestly status becomes theologically significant in v. 12, where he offers sacrifices to the God of Israel. The term choten ("father-in-law") is used specifically for the father of one's wife (as opposed to cham, "father-in-law" from the wife's perspective). The chapter uses choten Mosheh an extraordinary thirteen times, more than any other single descriptor in the chapter — the narrator insistently frames the entire episode through the lens of this kinship relationship. The clause ki-hotsi YHWH et-Yisra'el mimMitsrayim ("that the LORD brought out Israel from Egypt") uses the Hiphil of yatsa' ("to go out"), the fundamental Exodus verb — "to cause to go out, to bring out." Notably, v. 1a uses Elohim ("God") for the agent of deliverance, while v. 1b uses YHWH ("the LORD") — the narrator employs both divine names within a single verse, perhaps reflecting Jethro's perspective (Elohim, the generic divine name accessible to a Midianite priest) alongside the narrator's theological precision (YHWH, the covenant name revealed to Israel). LOGOS renders "Jethro" for Yitro, "priest of Midian" for kohen Midyan, and "father-in-law of Moses" for choten Mosheh.

2. **"After he had sent her away" (v. 2) — achar shillucheyha:** Hebrew vayyiqqach Yitro choten Mosheh et-Tsipporah eshet Mosheh achar shillucheyha. The phrase achar shillucheyha is enigmatic and theologically fraught. The noun shilluach (plural construct shilluchey, with 3fs suffix -ha) derives from the Piel of shalach, "to send away, to dismiss." In legal contexts, the Piel shillach is the technical term for divorce (Deut. 24:1, 3; Isa. 50:1; Jer. 3:8 — ki-shalach; sefer keritat, "document of cutting off, bill of divorce"). The phrase achar shillucheyha therefore can mean either "after her being sent away" (a neutral dismissal, perhaps for safety during the plagues and exodus) or "after her divorce" (a legal dissolution of marriage). The narrative provides no context for when or why this sending away occurred. In Exodus 4:24–26, the circumcision crisis at the lodging place on the way to Egypt is the last time Zipporah appears before this chapter — some traditions (Mekhilta de-Rabbi Ishmael, Yitro 1) understand Aaron to have met Moses at the mountain of God (4:27) and counseled him to send Zipporah and the sons back to Midian before entering Egypt, reasoning that there were already enough afflicted people in Egypt without adding more. The word shillucheyha ("her sending away") leaves the matter deliberately ambiguous — it is a sending, but the reason and circumstances are unnarrated. LOGOS renders "after he had sent her away" for achar shillucheyha, preserving the ambiguity. The LXX renders meta tēn aphesin autēs ("after her release/dismissal"), using aphesis, a word that in other contexts means "release" (of debts, of slaves) and in the New Testament, "forgiveness."

3. **"Gershom" and "Eliezer" (vv. 3–4) — Gershom, Eli'ezer:** The two sons of Moses are named with folk etymologies. Gershom is explained by ger hayiti be'erets nokhriyyah ("I was a sojourner in a foreign land") — the name is heard as ger-sham ("sojourner there") or ger-shom ("sojourner-name"), though the actual etymology may be different (possibly "expulsion" from garash, "to drive out"). The second son, Eliezer (Eli'ezer, "my God is help"), is given an etymology: ki-Elohey avi be'ezri vayyatsileni mecherev Par'oh ("for the God of my father was my help, and He delivered me from the sword of Pharaoh"). The "sword of Pharaoh" (cherev Par'oh) refers to the death sentence pronounced after Moses killed the Egyptian (Exod. 2:15). Notably, Eliezer's birth is not narrated in Exodus 2 — only Gershom's birth is recorded (2:22). Eliezer appears here for the first time, and apart from 1 Chronicles 23:15, 17, he plays no further role in the biblical narrative. The verb vayyatsileni ("and He delivered me," Hiphil of natsal, "to snatch away, to deliver") is the same verb used of Israel's deliverance throughout this chapter (vv. 8, 9, 10), creating a parallel between Moses' personal deliverance and Israel's national deliverance. The etiological notice for Gershom — "I was a sojourner in a foreign land" — is also recorded at 2:22, but the notice for Eliezer appears only here, as though the narrator saved this etymology for the moment when it would resonate most powerfully: at the reunion with Jethro, Moses can now look back and name his son's name as fulfilled prophecy. LOGOS renders both names without translation, providing the etymologies in brackets.

4. **"And Jethro rejoiced" (v. 9) — vayyichad Yitro:** Hebrew vayyichad Yitro al kol-hattovah asher-asah YHWH le'Yisra'el asher hitsilo miyyad Mitsrayim. The verb vayyichad is a hapax legomenon in this form — it occurs only here in the Hebrew Bible. Its derivation is disputed. The most common interpretation derives it from chadah, "to rejoice, to be glad" (cognate with Aramaic chadi, "to rejoice"), yielding "and Jethro rejoiced." However, an alternative interpretation, found in the Talmud (Sanhedrin 94a), derives it from chad, "sharp" — "his skin became prickly" or "he felt a chill," suggesting that Jethro experienced mixed emotions: joy over Israel's deliverance but also distress over Egypt's destruction, since Jethro himself was a non-Israelite and may have had connections to the defeated peoples. The Midrash (Mekhilta) records both interpretations: Rabbi Yehoshua says it means "he rejoiced"; Rabbi Elazar HaModa'i says it means "his flesh got goose-bumps." The phrase al kol-hattovah ("over all the good") uses tovah ("good, goodness, benefit") — a comprehensive term encompassing all the divine acts of provision and deliverance Moses has just recounted. LOGOS renders "rejoiced" for vayyichad, following the primary lexical tradition. The LXX renders exestē ("he was amazed, astonished, beside himself"), reading neither simple joy nor discomfort but rather overwhelming astonishment — a third interpretive possibility.

5. **"Now I know that the LORD is greater than all the gods" (v. 11) — attah yada'ti ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim:** Hebrew vayyomer Yitro attah yada'ti ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim ki vaddavar asher zadu aleyhem. This is Jethro's confession — the theological climax of the chapter. The adverb attah ("now") marks a turning point in knowledge: what Jethro formerly did not know (or only suspected), he now knows with certainty. The verb yada'ti ("I know," from yada', "to know") in the perfect tense indicates completed, settled knowledge. The comparative ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim ("that the LORD is great above all the gods") uses the preposition min ("from, more than") with ha'elohim ("the gods") to assert YHWH's supremacy. This is not monotheism in the strict philosophical sense — it does not deny the existence of other gods but declares YHWH's superiority over them. It is what scholars call "henotheism" or "monolatry" — the worship of one God as supreme while acknowledging the existence (or perceived existence) of others. The second clause, ki vaddavar asher zadu aleyhem ("indeed, in the matter in which they acted presumptuously against them"), is notoriously difficult. The verb zadu (from zud or zid, "to act presumptuously, to act insolently, to boil over") occurs in contexts of arrogant overreaching. The subject of zadu is unclear: it may be the Egyptians who acted presumptuously against Israel, or it may be the gods of Egypt who acted presumptuously. The preposition aleyhem ("against them") is likewise ambiguous: against Israel, or against the Israelites, or against the Egyptians themselves (in a boomerang of divine justice). The Talmud (Sotah 11a) reads a measure-for-measure principle: "In the very matter in which they [the Egyptians] plotted against them [Israel], they were judged" — the Egyptians drowned Israel's sons in the Nile, and God drowned the Egyptians in the Sea. LOGOS renders the verse literally, preserving the ambiguity. The LXX renders dioti epethento autois ("because they set upon them"), apparently reading "they attacked them" rather than "they acted presumptuously."

6. **"Burnt offering and sacrifices for God" (v. 12) — olah uzevachim le'lohim:** Hebrew vayyiqqach Yitro choten Mosheh olah uzevachim le'lohim vayyavo Aharon vekhol ziqney Yisra'el le'ekhol-lechem im-choten Mosheh lifney ha'Elohim. This verse is theologically remarkable: a Midianite priest offers sacrifices to the God of Israel, and Aaron and the elders of Israel participate in a communal meal with him "before God" (lifney ha'Elohim). The olah ("burnt offering," from alah, "to go up" — the offering that "goes up" entirely in smoke to God) is the most complete form of sacrifice, in which the entire animal is consumed on the altar. The zevachim ("sacrifices," plural of zevach, from zavach, "to slaughter, to sacrifice") are communion/peace offerings, portions of which are eaten by the worshipers in a shared meal. The phrase le'ekhol-lechem ("to eat bread/food") describes the communal meal that accompanies the zevachim — the sacrificial feast. That this meal takes place lifney ha'Elohim ("before God") means it occurs in the divine presence, probably at or near the altar. The scene has far-reaching implications for the relationship between Israelite worship and non-Israelite piety: Jethro, a Midianite priest, presides over or at least initiates a sacrifice that Aaron (Israel's future high priest) and the elders attend. Some scholars see this as evidence of a "Kenite hypothesis" — that Yahwistic worship originated among the Midianites/Kenites and was transmitted to Israel through Moses' marriage into Jethro's family. Whether or not this theory is correct, the narrative presents the scene without any hint of impropriety: Jethro's sacrifice is accepted, the meal is shared, and the divine presence is affirmed. Notably, Moses is not mentioned as participating in the meal — only Aaron and the elders — which may be a narrative gap or may suggest that Moses was occupied elsewhere. LOGOS renders "burnt offering and sacrifices" for olah uzevachim and "before God" for lifney ha'Elohim.

7. **"You will surely wear out" (v. 18) — navol tibbol:** Hebrew navol tibbol gam-attah gam-ha'am hazzeh asher immakh ki-khaved mimmekha haddavar lo-tukhal asohu levaddekha. The infinitive absolute construction navol tibbol (from navel, "to wilt, to wither, to fade, to wear out") creates emphatic certainty: "you will surely/certainly wear out." The verb navel is used of leaves withering (Isa. 1:30; 34:4; Jer. 8:13), of flowers fading (Isa. 28:1, 4; 40:7–8), and metaphorically of people languishing or collapsing from exhaustion (Exod. 18:18; 1 Sam. 22:5 — lo tevel, "lest you wear out"; Ps. 1:3 — lo yibbol, "it does not wither"). Jethro's warning extends beyond Moses alone: gam-attah gam-ha'am hazzeh asher immakh ("both you and also this people that is with you") — the current system damages not only the judge but the judged. The reason is stated with the keyword khaved ("heavy," from the root k-v-d, "to be heavy, to be weighty"): ki-khaved mimmekha haddavar ("for the matter is too heavy for you"). The root k-v-d has appeared throughout Exodus in connection with Pharaoh's "heavy" (kaved) heart and with the "glory" (kavod) of God — here it describes the crushing weight of judicial responsibility. The final clause lo-tukhal asohu levaddekha ("you cannot do it alone") uses levaddekha ("by yourself alone," from bad, "alone, separated"), the same word that appeared in v. 14 (maddua attah yoshev levaddekha, "why do you sit alone"). Jethro diagnoses the structural problem: not that Moses is incompetent, but that the task exceeds the capacity of any single person. LOGOS renders "you will surely wear out" for navol tibbol.

8. **"Men of ability" (v. 21) — anshey-chayil:** Hebrew vechatah mikkol-ha'am anshey-chayil yir'ey Elohim anshey emet son'ey vatsa vesamta aleyhem sarey alafim sarey me'ot sarey chamishim vesarey asarot. The term anshey-chayil ("men of chayil") is one of the most semantically rich expressions in biblical Hebrew. The noun chayil encompasses: (a) military valor and prowess (cf. gibbor chayil, "mighty warrior," Judg. 6:12; 11:1); (b) wealth and material resources (cf. chayil in the sense of "wealth," Deut. 8:17–18); (c) moral excellence and virtue (cf. eshet-chayil, "woman of valor/worth," Prov. 31:10; Ruth 3:11); and (d) administrative competence and capability (the sense here). In the context of judicial appointment, chayil denotes the comprehensive fitness required of a judge: courage, integrity, capability, and resourcefulness. Jethro prescribes four qualifications: (1) anshey-chayil — men of ability/valor; (2) yir'ey Elohim — fearers of God (yir'ah, "fear, reverence, awe" — the foundational religious disposition); (3) anshey emet — men of truth (emet, "truth, reliability, faithfulness" — from the root aman, the same root as emunah in 17:12); (4) son'ey vatsa — haters of unjust gain (betsa, "profit, unjust gain, bribery" — from batsa', "to cut off, to gain by violence"). These four criteria move from general competence to specific ethical commitments: capability, piety, integrity, incorruptibility. The hierarchical structure — sarey alafim, sarey me'ot, sarey chamishim, sarey asarot ("rulers of thousands, of hundreds, of fifties, of tens") — reflects a decimal-based military organization that also functions as a civilian administrative system. The sar ("ruler, official, chief" — from sarar, "to rule, to be chief") is a flexible title applicable to both military and administrative contexts. LOGOS renders "men of ability" for anshey-chayil, "fearers of God" for yir'ey Elohim, "men of truth" for anshey emet, and "haters of unjust gain" for son'ey vatsa. The LXX renders andras dunatous ("capable men"), theosebeis ("God-fearing"), andras dikaious ("righteous men"), and misountas huperēphanian ("hating arrogance") — notably rendering emet ("truth") as dikaios ("righteous") and betsa ("unjust gain") as huperēphania ("arrogance"), a significant interpretive shift.

9. **"Rulers of thousands" (v. 21) — sarey alafim:** The four-tier judicial hierarchy — thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens — appears also in Deuteronomy 1:15, where Moses recounts this same event in his farewell speech, though with slight variations (Deuteronomy adds "officers" — shoterim — and does not mention Jethro's initiative, attributing the idea to Moses' own recognition of the burden). The noun elef ("thousand") may in some contexts refer not to the numeral 1,000 but to a clan or military unit (cf. the use of elef/aluf in Judges and the census numbers). If so, sarey alafim would mean "chiefs of clans/units" rather than "rulers of [groups of] one thousand people." However, the graduated scale — thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens — suggests numerical groupings. This decimal system of judicial organization has parallels in other ancient Near Eastern administrative systems, including Egyptian and Mesopotamian provincial governance structures. The system is designed for scalability: disputes are resolved at the lowest competent level, and only cases beyond the capacity of lower officials ascend to higher authorities, ultimately reaching Moses himself for only the most difficult matters. LOGOS renders "rulers of thousands" for sarey alafim.

10. **"The hard matter... every small matter" (v. 26) — haddavar haqqasheh... haddavar haqqaton:** Hebrew veshaftu et-ha'am bekhol-et et-haddavar haqqasheh yevi'un el-Mosheh vekhol-haddavar haqqaton yishpetu hem. In v. 22 (Jethro's proposal), the contrast is between haddavar haggadol ("the great/large matter") and haddavar haqqaton ("the small matter"). In v. 26 (Moses' implementation), the contrast shifts to haddavar haqqasheh ("the hard/difficult matter") and haddavar haqqaton ("the small matter"). The change from gadol ("great") to qasheh ("hard, difficult") is subtle but significant: Jethro framed the distinction by scale (great vs. small), while the narrator frames it by difficulty (hard vs. small). A "great" matter is one involving important people or large stakes; a "hard" matter is one requiring wisdom, precedent, or direct divine consultation. The implementation thus refines the criterion for escalation: it is not the social importance of the disputants that sends a case to Moses, but the legal complexity of the case itself. The adjective qasheh ("hard, difficult, severe, stiff" — from qashah, "to be hard") is the same word used of Pharaoh's "hard" heart (Exod. 7:3, haqsheh) and of the "hard" servitude (Exod. 1:14, qashah). LOGOS preserves both terms: "great matter" in v. 22 and "hard matter" in v. 26.

11. **"And he went to his land" (v. 27) — vayyyelekh lo el-artso:** Hebrew vayeshalach Mosheh et-chotno vayyyelekh lo el-artso. The chapter closes with Jethro's departure. The verb vayeshalach ("and he sent away," Piel of shalach) echoes the shillucheyha ("her being sent away") of v. 2 — the same root governs both the earlier sending away of Zipporah and the final sending away of Jethro. The phrase vayyyelekh lo el-artso ("and he went for himself to his land") includes the reflexive/ethical dative lo ("for himself, on his own behalf"), suggesting purposeful, voluntary departure. The destination el-artso ("to his land") is Midian — Jethro returns to his own people, his own priesthood, his own land. This departure creates an interpretive question: in Numbers 10:29–32, Moses urges Chovav (identified by many as Jethro or Jethro's son) not to leave but to serve as Israel's "eyes" in the wilderness. The relationship between Exodus 18:27 (Jethro departs) and Numbers 10:29–32 (Moses urges him to stay) is debated — they may represent different moments, different traditions, or the same event told from different perspectives. LOGOS renders "and he went to him, to his land" — preserving the lo, though its meaning is debated (some read "to him" as "to his own [place]," while others read it as an ethical dative).

---

### Theological Note: The Outsider Who Teaches Israel

Exodus 18 is unique in the Pentateuch for placing the wisdom of a non-Israelite priest at the center of Israel's institutional formation. Jethro — a Midianite, a priest of a people who will later become enemies of Israel (Num. 25:6–18; 31:1–12) — arrives at the mountain of God, confesses the LORD's supremacy, offers sacrifices that Aaron and the elders share, and then prescribes the judicial system that will govern Israel for the remainder of its history. No Israelite leader proposes this system. No divine command initiates it. The administrative structure that will shape Israel's governance from Sinai onward originates in the counsel of a foreign priest.

The chapter's theological architecture moves through three phases: hearing (vv. 1–6), confessing (vv. 7–12), and counseling (vv. 13–27). In the first phase, Jethro hears (vayyishma, v. 1) what God has done — the same verb that will open the Shema (shema Yisra'el, Deut. 6:4). Hearing precedes faith; faith requires a report (cf. Rom. 10:17, "faith comes from hearing"). Jethro hears the report of the Exodus and responds by coming to Moses — bringing with him Moses' wife and sons, the family Moses had sent away. The reunion of Moses with his family, facilitated by his father-in-law, frames the entire chapter as a restoration: before the judicial reform, before the confession of faith, there is the return of a wife and children to a husband and father.

The second phase — Moses' recounting and Jethro's response (vv. 7–12) — builds to the theological climax in vv. 10–11. Jethro's blessing (barukh YHWH, "blessed be the LORD") is the response of a man who has heard and understood. His confession (attah yada'ti ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim, "now I know that the LORD is greater than all the gods") is striking for several reasons. First, it uses attah ("now"), marking a temporal and epistemological turning point — there was a before, when Jethro did not know or did not fully know, and there is a now, when knowledge has become certain. Second, it uses the comparative mikkol-ha'elohim ("above all the gods"), which acknowledges the existence (or at least the cultural reality) of other gods while declaring YHWH's supremacy. Third, it comes from the mouth of a kohen Midyan — a priest of another religion — making it a conversion narrative, or at least a recognition narrative: the foreign priest recognizes what the plagues and the sea were designed to demonstrate.

The enigmatic clause ki vaddavar asher zadu aleyhem ("indeed, in the matter in which they acted presumptuously against them") has been interpreted as a statement of measure-for-measure justice (middah keneged middah): the very instruments of Egyptian oppression became the instruments of Egyptian judgment. They drowned Hebrew children; their army drowned in the sea. They forced hard labor; their economy was destroyed by plagues. The word zadu ("they acted presumptuously") carries the connotation of boiling over, of insolent overreaching — the arrogance of empire that overplays its hand and discovers that the God of the slaves is the God of the universe.

The sacrificial meal of v. 12 is one of the most remarkable scenes in the Pentateuch. Jethro takes (vayyiqqach) a burnt offering and sacrifices "for God" (le'lohim) — the text does not say "to the LORD" (laYHWH) but "for God" (le'lohim), using the more universal divine name, perhaps reflecting Jethro's still-developing theological vocabulary. Aaron and all the elders of Israel come to eat bread with Jethro "before God" (lifney ha'Elohim) — a covenantal meal in the divine presence. The fact that Aaron participates is especially notable: Aaron, who will become the high priest of Israel, eats sacrificial food offered by a Midianite priest. The meal takes place at "the mountain of God" (har ha'Elohim, v. 5), the same mountain where the covenant will be ratified and the law given. Before Sinai is the mountain of law, it is the mountain of a shared meal between Israel's leaders and a foreign priest who has confessed the LORD.

The third phase — the judicial reform (vv. 13–27) — is framed by two observations of isolation: Jethro sees Moses sitting "alone" (levaddekha, v. 14) and warns that he cannot do it "alone" (levaddekha, v. 18). The word levaddekha ("by yourself alone") echoes Genesis 2:18: lo-tov heyot ha'adam levaddo ("it is not good for the man to be alone"). The structural parallel is deliberate: just as it was "not good" (lo-tov) for Adam to be alone and God provided a helper, so it is "not good" (lo-tov, v. 17) for Moses to judge alone and Jethro provides a system. The helper in Genesis 2 is the woman; the helpers in Exodus 18 are the appointed judges. The solution to aloneness is community; the solution to overwork is delegation; the solution to one man's burden is shared responsibility.

Jethro's four criteria for the judges — men of ability, fearers of God, men of truth, haters of unjust gain (v. 21) — constitute a comprehensive ethical profile. Chayil ("ability") ensures competence; yir'at Elohim ("fear of God") ensures accountability to a higher authority; emet ("truth") ensures integrity in judgment; sin'at betsa ("hatred of unjust gain") ensures incorruptibility. The order matters: competence comes first (an incompetent but pious judge is still a bad judge), followed by piety (competence without reverence for God becomes mere bureaucracy), followed by truthfulness (reverence without commitment to truth becomes sentimentality), followed by incorruptibility (truth without resistance to bribery becomes theoretical). The four qualities form a complete judicial character: able, God-fearing, truthful, incorruptible.

The hierarchical structure — thousands, hundreds, fifties, tens — distributes justice throughout the community so that every person has access to a judge at the most local level. The system is pyramidal: the vast majority of disputes are resolved at the level of tens, where the judge knows the parties personally. Only cases too difficult for local resolution ascend through the hierarchy. Moses remains at the apex, but he now handles only the "hard matters" (haddavar haqqasheh, v. 26) — the precedent-setting cases, the cases requiring direct divine consultation (lidrosh Elohim, v. 15). The system protects Moses from exhaustion and the people from delay; it also protects justice itself, since a judge overwhelmed by volume cannot give adequate attention to any single case.

The chapter's conclusion — Jethro's departure to his own land (v. 27) — creates a poignant frame. The foreign priest arrives, confesses the LORD, shares a meal with Israel's leaders, prescribes the governmental system that will serve Israel for centuries, and then leaves. He returns to Midian, to his own people, to his own priesthood. He does not join Israel. He does not stay for Sinai. He does not receive the Torah. The wisdom he contributes becomes permanent; his presence does not. The chapter thus raises a profound question about the relationship between divine truth and human vessels: God's governance of Israel is shaped in part by the counsel of a man who worships at another altar. The boundary between insider and outsider, between Israel and the nations, is not as impermeable as later tradition will sometimes suggest. At the mountain of God, before the giving of the law, a Midianite priest taught Israel how to administer justice — and then went home.

The placement of Exodus 18 between the Amalek battle (ch. 17) and the Sinai revelation (ch. 19) is narratively and theologically significant. After the warfare of chapter 17 and before the theophany of chapter 19, the text inserts a chapter about governance, administration, and the practical structures of communal life. The sequence suggests that revelation requires preparation: before Israel can receive the law, it must have a system for administering it. Jethro's judicial reform is not an interruption of the Sinai narrative — it is its precondition. The people cannot stand before God at Sinai without first having a structure for standing before human judges in their camps. The infrastructure of justice precedes the reception of law.

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 18 (pages 59–60) preserves the LXX version with several notable features. At v. 1, the LXX renders Yitro as Iothor and kohen Midyan as ho hiereus Madiam, using hiereus ("priest") for kohen. At v. 2, the LXX renders achar shillucheyha as meta tēn aphesin autēs ("after her release"), using aphesis — a word that in the New Testament becomes the standard term for "forgiveness" of sins, but here retains its primary sense of "sending away, release." At v. 5, the LXX specifies to oros tou theou ("the mountain of God") for har ha'Elohim. At v. 9, the LXX renders the hapax vayyichad as exestē ("he was amazed"), reading astonishment rather than joy — a divergence that changes the emotional register of Jethro's response from delight to overwhelming wonder. At v. 11, the LXX renders the comparative ki-gadol YHWH mikkol-ha'elohim as hoti megas kurios para pantas tous theous ("because the Lord is great beyond all the gods"), using para with the accusative for the Hebrew min. The difficult clause ki vaddavar asher zadu aleyhem is rendered dioti epethento autois ("because they set upon them"), apparently reading the presumptuous action as a military attack rather than as arrogant overreaching. At v. 12, the LXX renders olah as holokautōma ("whole burnt offering") and zevachim as thusias ("sacrifices"), and preserves the crucial phrase lifney ha'Elohim as enantion tou theou ("in the presence of God"). At v. 17, the LXX renders lo-tov as ou orthōs ("not rightly"), shifting the evaluation from "not good" (moral) to "not correct" (procedural). At v. 18, the LXX preserves the infinitive absolute with phthora kataphthareisē ("with wearing you will wear out"). At v. 21, the LXX's rendering of the four judicial qualifications is significant: anshey-chayil becomes andras dunatous ("capable men"), yir'ey Elohim becomes theosebeis ("God-fearing"), anshey emet becomes andras dikaious ("righteous men" — rendering "truth" as "righteousness"), and son'ey vatsa becomes misountas huperēphanian ("hating arrogance" — rendering "unjust gain" as "arrogance/pride"). The shift from "truth" to "righteousness" and from "unjust gain" to "arrogance" represents an interpretive transformation: the LXX moves from the Hebrew's concrete, judicial virtues to more abstract, philosophical categories. At v. 27, the LXX renders el-artso as eis tēn gēn autou ("into his land"), preserving the Hebrew closely. Throughout the Vaticanus pages 59–60, the text is written in the standard three-column continuous uncial script, with Exodus 18 beginning on the lower portion of page 59 and continuing through page 60.

--- LOGOS
